author: @magnuswootton7368
text: it doesnt matter how big the square is,  cause its l/w any square size is good for this measurement.

author: @agentbertram4769
text: Thanks Rob.  This helps me to make a test block that will give consistent and meaningful results.

author: @Vibranium603
text: Is it correct to say sheet resistance of a conductive layer printed on porous substrate is difficult to measure? can line resistance be measured instead?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Arvind G noo it's easy to measure - but a little difficult to interpret. If the substrate absorbed the film then how thick is the film? that can be a bit difficult to answer

replies.author: @Vibranium603
replies.text: So there is no point using four probe method on the porous substrate?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Arvind G you can do if you want - you just have to take account of it - it's just easier to do a non porous substrate as it is one variable out of the equation

author: @rsa4510
text: Robert, nice clear explanation.  One thing I was not 100% clear on is why it doesn't seem to matter much what the thickness is for a film.  There must be a threshold where you can ignore that for some reason?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +rsa4510 there is mate - films divide into thick films, thin films and solid material - the definition is a little grey  - a thin film is monoatomic layer to a few microns, a thick film can be quite a few microns thick and refers to paint and ink coatings mostly - as i say it is a guideline as no one has really specified what the film thickness is

author: @VAXKILLEDMYDAD
text: You are a great teacher! A very clear explanation, and I'm glad to hear you're getting results that are beating the commercial options out there.
